title: Affogato
subtitle: A scoop of vanilla ice cream drowned in a shot of hot espresso
excerpt: Two scoops of vanilla ice cream in a chilled glass, immediately covered with a freshly made double shot of very hot, very strong espresso. The ice cream begins to melt at the edges into the coffee; the coffee is cooled by the ice cream. Serve the moment the espresso is poured.
sourceNotes: Affogato al caffè is an Italian dessert or coffee preparation, depending on how strictly you define the categories, that appears in Italian cafés and restaurants as an alternative to a post-meal coffee. The name means 'drowned'. The ice cream drowns in the coffee. The technique requires no cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cannot be made ahead. The affogato must be served and eaten immediately.

content.text: An affogato is a question of temperature and timing. The espresso must be very hot, freshly made, and the ice cream must be very cold. The coffee poured over cold ice cream creates a brief moment where the edges melt and the two temperatures meet in the glass. This lasts about three minutes before it becomes simply a coffee with melted ice cream. Serve immediately after pouring.

Use the best vanilla ice cream you have. Bought ice cream works if it is a good brand; the vanilla ice cream recipe in this collection is the better choice. The coffee is a double espresso per person, made as strong and hot as your machine allows. Weak or lukewarm coffee produces a disappointing result.

items.prepNote: dissolved in 100 ml of just-boiled water (or use 100 ml of freshly brewed espresso)

content.text: You will also need 4 scoops of vanilla ice cream, use the vanilla ice cream recipe in this collection or a good-quality bought brand. Chill two serving glasses or small bowls in the freezer for 10 minutes before assembling.

Place 2 scoops of vanilla ice cream in each chilled glass. Make the espresso immediately before serving, do not let it sit and cool. Pour 50 ml of hot espresso over the ice cream in each glass. Serve within 30 seconds. Provide a long spoon; the correct way to eat an affogato is to eat the ice cream and drink the coffee-cream liquid at the bottom of the glass.

items.symptom: Ice cream melted completely before serving
items.cause: Ice cream was allowed to soften before assembly, or coffee sat too long before pouring
items.fix: Keep the ice cream in the freezer until the moment of assembly. Make the espresso and pour immediately. The whole process from scoop to table should take under a minute.

items.cause: Coffee was not strong enough or was too much water to coffee ratio
items.fix: Use 4 teaspoons of espresso powder per 100 ml of water for a very strong result. A freshly pulled espresso shot from a machine is the best option if available.

items.cause: Ice cream was not a good-quality vanilla or had too much air
items.fix: Use a custard-based ice cream with a high egg yolk content. Supermarket 'economy' ice creams are very aerated and dilute the coffee rather than enriching it.

items.symptom: Nothing happened, ice cream didn't melt
items.fix: The coffee must be freshly made and very hot. Coffee that has sat for even 2 minutes will have cooled enough to not melt the ice cream properly.

content.text: Affogato al caffè sits somewhere between dessert and coffee in the Italian understanding of what it is. In Italian cafés it is typically served at the end of a meal as an alternative to a plain espresso, and it appears on both the dessert menu and the coffee menu of many Italian restaurants. The dish requires no cooking and no technique beyond brewing espresso, it is, in its essentials, a coffee preparation that happens to involve ice cream. It arrived in British restaurants with the wave of Italian food culture in the late twentieth century and is now a reliable item on Italian restaurant menus.
